_meta.description: GraphQL vulnerability knowledge base for APISec scanner.

introspection.label: Introspection Exposed
introspection.impact: Attacker can map the entire API schema — queries, mutations, types and field names — without any credentials.
introspection.description: GraphQL introspection is enabled in production. It allows any client to query the full schema structure, exposing all available operations and data models. This significantly accelerates targeted attacks by removing the reconnaissance barrier.
introspection.solution: Disable introspection in production. If needed for internal tooling, restrict it to trusted IP ranges or require authentication. Use Apollo Server's 'introspection: false' or the equivalent for your framework.

bypass.label: Introspection Bypass
bypass.impact: Schema leak despite introspection being supposedly disabled. Attacker can reconstruct the schema using GET requests or newline injection.
bypass.description: The server blocks introspection on POST requests but still responds to introspection queries sent via GET or with a newline character injected in the query string. This is a common misconfiguration in servers that use naive keyword-based blocking.
bypass.solution: Block introspection at the parser level, not via string matching. Ensure the restriction applies to all HTTP methods (GET, POST) and query formats.

fields.label: Sensitive Field Exposure
fields.owasp: API3:2023 - Broken Object Property Level Authorization
fields.impact: Sensitive data fields (password, token, secret, apiKey) are declared in the schema and may return real values to any authenticated or unauthenticated caller.
fields.description: The GraphQL schema exposes fields with sensitive names (password, token, secret, apiKey, ssn, creditCard, etc.). Even if these fields return null for some users, their presence in the schema is a security concern and may indicate insufficient field-level access control.
fields.solution: Remove sensitive fields from the public schema. Implement field-level authorization resolvers. Consider using schema masking or persisted queries to limit schema exposure.

auth.label: Broken Auth on Mutations
auth.impact: Unauthenticated attackers can execute privileged mutations — delete accounts, escalate roles, reset passwords — without any credentials.
auth.description: Dangerous mutations (deleteUser, updateRole, resetPassword, createAdmin, etc.) are accessible without a valid authentication token. The server does not enforce authentication middleware on these operations, allowing any caller to perform privileged actions.
auth.solution: Apply authentication middleware globally on all mutations. Use a schema directive (@auth) or resolver-level guards. Never rely on mutation naming conventions to infer privilege requirements.

idor.label: IDOR via GraphQL Queries
idor.impact: Any user can access any other user's data by supplying an arbitrary object ID in a query argument, bypassing ownership checks.
idor.description: GraphQL queries accept arbitrary object IDs (user, post, order, account) without verifying that the requesting user is authorized to access the requested object. This is a classic Insecure Direct Object Reference vulnerability adapted to GraphQL's query structure.
idor.solution: Implement object-level authorization in every resolver. Verify that the authenticated user owns or has permission to access the requested object. Do not rely solely on authentication — authorization must be enforced per-object.

csrf.label: CSRF via GET or text/plain
csrf.impact: An attacker can craft a malicious page that triggers a GraphQL mutation on behalf of a logged-in victim, performing account changes or data deletion without user interaction.
csrf.description: The GraphQL endpoint accepts mutations via HTTP GET requests or via POST with Content-Type: text/plain. Browsers do not send CORS preflight requests for these methods, making the endpoint vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery. A malicious website can silently submit mutations as the victim.
csrf.solution: Restrict the GraphQL endpoint to POST requests only. Enforce Content-Type: application/json. Implement CSRF tokens or SameSite cookie attributes. Validate the Origin and Referer headers.

sqli.label: SQL Injection in Arguments
sqli.owasp: API3:2023 - Broken Object Property Level Authorization
sqli.impact: Full database compromise — data exfiltration, authentication bypass, data manipulation, and potentially remote code execution depending on the database engine.
sqli.description: GraphQL arguments of type String are passed directly to SQL queries without parameterization or sanitization. Classic SQL injection payloads (OR 1=1, UNION SELECT, time-based) are accepted and executed by the underlying database.
sqli.solution: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements exclusively. Never interpolate GraphQL argument values directly into SQL strings. Apply input validation at the resolver level.

nosqli.label: NoSQL Injection in Arguments
nosqli.owasp: API3:2023 - Broken Object Property Level Authorization
nosqli.impact: Authentication bypass, unauthorized data access, and full collection enumeration via MongoDB operators ($where, $gt, $regex, $ne).
nosqli.description: GraphQL arguments that accept object or filter types are passed unsanitized to a NoSQL database. MongoDB operators injected via JSON input ($where, $gt, $ne, $regex) are interpreted by the query engine, enabling authentication bypass and data exfiltration.
nosqli.solution: Sanitize all input objects. Strip or reject keys starting with '$'. Use schema validation and allow-list field names. Disable server-side JavaScript execution in MongoDB.

batch.label: Batch Query Attack
batch.impact: Rate-limit bypass allowing credential brute-force, enumeration, and server overload (DoS) — sending 50+ operations in a single HTTP request.
batch.description: The GraphQL endpoint accepts batched queries — an array of query objects in a single HTTP request — without any limit on batch size. Attackers can bundle dozens or hundreds of operations into one request, effectively multiplying throughput while appearing as a single HTTP call to rate-limiting systems.
batch.solution: Disable query batching if not required. If needed, enforce a strict maximum batch size (e.g. 5-10). Apply rate limiting per operation count, not per HTTP request.

alias.label: Alias Attack
alias.impact: Rate-limit bypass — an attacker can attempt dozens of passwords or enumeration values in a single HTTP request using GraphQL aliases.
alias.description: The GraphQL endpoint accepts queries containing a large number of aliases (e.g. q1: login(password:'a'), q2: login(password:'b'), ...) without restriction. Since all aliases are resolved within a single HTTP request, per-request rate limiting is ineffective and brute-force attacks become trivial.
alias.solution: Implement alias count limiting (max 10 per request). Apply rate limiting per operation resolved, not per HTTP request. Use query complexity analysis.

depth.label: Depth Attack (DoS)
depth.impact: Server-side DoS — deeply nested queries force the resolver to perform exponential work, potentially exhausting CPU and memory.
depth.description: The GraphQL server accepts queries nested to an arbitrary depth (10+ levels) without enforcing a maximum depth limit. Deeply nested queries cause exponential resolver invocations, leading to resource exhaustion and denial of service for legitimate users.
depth.solution: Implement query depth limiting (recommended max: 5-7 levels). Use libraries like graphql-depth-limit (Node.js) or graphene-django's depth validation. Combine with query complexity analysis.

subscription.label: Subscription Abuse
subscription.impact: Unauthenticated real-time data streaming — an attacker can subscribe to live events (messages, transactions, user activity) without credentials.
subscription.description: GraphQL subscription endpoints (WebSocket-based) do not enforce authentication or authorization during the subscription handshake. Unauthenticated clients can subscribe to sensitive event streams and receive real-time data that should be restricted.
subscription.solution: Enforce authentication during the WebSocket connection handshake and on each subscription resolver. Apply the same authorization rules to subscriptions as to queries and mutations.

error.label: Error Disclosure
error.impact: Internal details exposed in error messages — stack traces, file paths, database queries, framework versions — aid attacker reconnaissance.
error.description: The GraphQL server returns verbose error messages containing stack traces, internal file paths, database error strings, or framework version information. This information is valuable for attackers profiling the backend technology stack and crafting targeted exploits.
error.solution: Set the server to production mode to suppress stack traces. Return generic error messages to clients. Log detailed errors server-side only. Use a dedicated error masking middleware.
